output:
# POOL and PRISM: Treating Sequential Structure as a Per-User Order-Relevance Field instead of a Global Dataset-Level Constant in Sequential-Recommendation Evaluation

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Sequential Recommender Systems (SRSs) predict a user's next interaction from the ORDER of their past interactions, on the premise that "the order of interactions and sequential patterns play an essential role" [1]. The field has converged on a dominant evaluation recipe: pick a handful of public datasets, train a sequential model -- canonically SASRec [2] or GRU4Rec [3], more recently BERT4Rec [4], Caser [5], and their contrastive/temporal descendants -- under leave-one-out next-item prediction, and report a single pooled ranking number (NDCG@k, HitRate@k, MRR) per dataset. This recipe is what the sequence-aware recommendation line [6] is built on, and a wave of methodological-revision papers has interrogated almost every part of it: the models themselves [10, 11], the loss and negative sampling [11], evaluation protocols and data leakage [7, 13], data-splitting strategies [15], and the broader "are we really making progress?" reproducibility concern [17].

One part of the recipe was, until very recently, taken entirely on faith: the DATASET. A sequential model can only be credited for "using order" if the data it is evaluated on actually contains order-dependent structure -- the "dataset-task mismatch" problem named by Hidasi and Czapp [7]. The source paper of this analysis, Klenitskiy, Volodkevich, Pembek and Vasilev's "Does It Look Sequential?" [1], is the first systematic attempt to MEASURE that structure across the benchmarks the community actually uses. Its instrument is the SHUFFLE TEST: because randomly permuting the items inside each user's history explicitly destroys sequential dependencies, the strength of sequential structure can be estimated by how much a metric DROPS when the data is shuffled. The paper operationalizes this with three approaches: (i) a model-agnostic count of sequential association rules before vs. after shuffling [5, and the rule-mining tradition]; (ii) the relative drop in a trained SASRec/GRU4Rec's NDCG@k and HitRate@k when the SAME trained model is applied to shuffled vs. original TEST sequences -- a faster variant of Woolridge et al.'s retrain-on-shuffled protocol [8]; and (iii) the top-K Jaccard "Rank List Sensitivity" [9] between the recommendation lists produced on original vs. shuffled histories. Curating the 15 most frequently used benchmarks from 130 RecSys/SIGIR/CIKM papers (2019-2023) plus three industrial datasets (Zvuk, MegaMarket, OTTO), it reports a striking verdict: several heavily used datasets -- Foursquare, Gowalla, RetailRocket, Steam, and Yelp -- have a "rather weak sequential structure," and are therefore questionable choices for SRS evaluation [1].

The concrete numbers make the case vivid. Reading the SASRec NDCG@10 relative drop under test-sequence shuffling [1, Table 2], the datasets span a wide spectrum: from strongly sequential -- ML-20m -59%, MegaMarket -47%, Amazon-Beauty -39%, Amazon-Games -33%, OTTO -30%, Amazon-Sports -28%, Yoochoose -22% -- down to a group where shuffling barely moves the metric: Steam -10%, Gowalla -8%, Foursquare -5%, RetailRocket -4%, and Yelp, where shuffling the order did not even hurt SASRec NDCG (about +5%). A bootstrap significance test leads the paper to flag SIX datasets -- Diginetica, Foursquare, Gowalla, RetailRocket, Steam, Yelp -- as lacking statistically significant sequential structure [1] (the Introduction's five, plus Diginetica). The top-K Jaccard tells the same story inverted (Steam 0.59, Diginetica 0.52, RetailRocket 0.47 are highly STABLE under shuffling, i.e. weakly sequential, whereas ML-20m 0.12, MegaMarket 0.19, Games 0.22 are highly sensitive, i.e. strongly sequential). The contribution is real and overdue: it turns "is this dataset sequential?" from an unspoken assumption into a measured quantity, and it gives practitioners a sanity check before they invest in a sequential model.

The silent assumption that survives INSIDE this new instrument is the subject of the present work. Every one of the three shuffle-based measures is a DATASET-LEVEL SCALAR: one drop, one Jaccard, one rule-ratio per dataset, each obtained by averaging over the entire user population. The verdict "Steam is weakly sequential (-10%)" is a single number standing in for millions of users. This treats a dataset's sequentiality as a GLOBAL CONSTANT -- a property of the dataset as a whole -- when it is in truth a property that varies enormously from user to user (a binge-watcher who consumes a series in order vs. a sporadic browser of unrelated items) and even within a single user's history (a tightly coupled session embedded in months of noise). Positioned against the surrounding literature, this gap is unfilled on every side: the shuffle-test line [1, 8] reports global scalars; the order-ablation line [7] compares a sequential model to its bag-of-items variant at the dataset level; the rank-sensitivity line [9] aggregates Jaccard over all users; the dataset-characterization line [16] clusters whole datasets by global descriptors; and the evaluation-protocol line [7, 13, 15, 17] debates splits and leakage, not per-user heterogeneity of the signal being measured. Nobody has asked WHERE inside a dataset the sequential structure lives -- and that is exactly the question this proposal makes measurable.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the gap POOL (Pooling Obscures Order-relevance at the user Level). The shuffle test defines a dataset-level statistic S(D) -- the relative drop of a pooled metric under shuffling -- and implicitly asserts that this single number characterizes "how sequential D is." POOL states that S(D) is merely the POPULATION MEAN of a heterogeneous PER-USER ORDER-RELEVANCE FIELD s: U -> [0,1], where s(u) is the degree to which the order of user u's history carries information about u's next item. Formally, because the pooled metric is itself an average over test users, M(pi, D) = (1/|U|) sum_u M_u(pi, H_u), the global drop is a metric-weighted mean of the per-user drops:

  S(D) = [ sum_u (M_u^orig - M_u^shuf) ] / [ sum_u M_u^orig ]  =  <metric-weighted mean over u of>  s(u),

so the deployed verdict S(D) is the s(.)-is-constant special case of a per-user analysis. Whenever the field is non-constant -- which it almost always is -- S(D) conceals its own shape.

The defining property of POOL is that the harm is SILENT under a pooled scalar and CONCENTRATES in the tails. A mean cannot distinguish a uniformly mildly-sequential dataset from a mixture of a strongly-sequential minority and a non-sequential majority; yet these demand opposite decisions. A dataset declared "weakly sequential" globally (Steam -10%, RetailRocket -4% [1]) can still contain a large sub-population of users for whom order is decisive -- users a sequential model SHOULD be credited for serving -- whose signal is diluted to near-zero by an inert majority (metric blindness). Symmetrically, a dataset declared "strongly sequential" (ML-20m -59% [1]) can owe its headline to a sequential sub-population while a substantial non-sequential remainder is being modeled with needless sequential machinery. The error is a MULTIPLIER on every downstream conclusion drawn from the global verdict: a benchmark discarded as "not sequential" discards its sequential sub-population too, and a model ranked on a pooled number is rewarded or punished for behavior on users where order does not even apply. The harm VANISHES if and only if the field is constant, s(u) == s_0 for all u: when every user is equally (non-)sequential, the mean tells the whole story and POOL disappears. The gap exists exactly because the field is non-constant -- and the source paper's own cross-dataset spread (-4% to -59% [1]) is strong prima facie evidence that the WITHIN-dataset spread is large too.

We introduce PRISM (Per-useR Indexed Sequentiality Measurement), which promotes the sequentiality scalar to a field and acts on it with two orthogonal knobs. A prism disperses one white-light beam (S(D)) into its spectrum (the field s(.)); PRISM does the same to the shuffle test. The field is estimated FOR FREE from signals the source pipeline already computes, just read PER USER instead of pooled: (e1) PER-USER SHUFFLE DELTA -- the relative drop of a model's per-user metric when only user u's test history is shuffled, computed with a circularity-safe CROSS-FIT or FROZEN teacher (never the live evaluated model), i.e. the source paper's own approach (ii) localized; (e2) PER-USER SEQUENTIAL-RULE COVERAGE -- the fraction of u's adjacent transitions explained by mined sequential rules with lift above a popularity/Markov-0 baseline, i.e. approach (i) localized, fully model-agnostic; and (e3) ORDER-AWARE-vs-ORDER-AGNOSTIC PREDICTABILITY GAP -- the per-user difference in next-item predictability (log-probability or reciprocal rank) between a sequential model pi and its order-agnostic twin pi_bag (the sequence-mixing block replaced by mean-pooling / a feedforward layer, exactly Hidasi and Czapp's ablation [7]). The raw estimate s_hat(u) -- a fusion of e1, e2, e3 -- is passed through a small held-out CALIBRATION map c(.), fit by isotonic or Platt scaling on a SEMI-SYNTHETIC PROBE in which per-user order-relevance is GROUND-TRUTH LABELED (sequential users generated from Markov chains, non-sequential users from i.i.d. popularity sampling), yielding s_cal(u) = c(s_hat(u)), anchored so that the metric-weighted population mean of s_cal equals S(D). KNOB1 (STRATIFIED EVALUATION / ROUTING): partition the test population by s_cal(u) and report metrics per stratum (the primary readout), and/or route each user with rho(u) = pi if s_cal(u) >= gamma else pi_bag/pop -- a per-user model selector. KNOB2 (REWEIGHTING): a per-user weight w(u) = phi(s_cal(u)) (phi monotone non-decreasing) yields a sequentiality-controlled metric that scores a model where order actually matters, and can reweight the training loss to focus sequential capacity on sequential users.

The decisive design property is the EXACT-COLLAPSE guarantee [provable-by-construction]. The collapse is a DESIGN SWITCH that disables estimation, not a claim that the true field is constant: set s_cal(u) == S(D) for all u (homogeneous default). Then stratification has a single stratum, so every stratified metric equals the pooled metric M(pi, D); the router rho is constant, so routing reduces to single-model evaluation; and the weights w(u) = phi(S(D)) are uniform, so the reweighted metric equals the unweighted pooled metric. Readout-by-readout, every PRISM output equals the corresponding source-paper dataset-level output (pooled M, global S(D), global Jaccard), with discrepancy E_collapse = max over readouts of | PRISM_readout - source_readout | <= 1e-6 enforced by a CI-gateable build check (a finite-precision implementation budget, not a performance claim). PRISM is therefore a STRICT, SAFE GENERALIZATION of the shuffle test: at the homogeneous default it IS "Does It Look Sequential?", and it can only ADD information.

The novelty wedge is four clauses that, taken together, no prior line covers more than two of: C1 -- sequential structure is modeled as a PER-USER (and per-interaction) FIELD s(u), s(u,t), not a global dataset-level constant (scalar -> field promotion); C2 -- the field is estimated FOR FREE from signals the shuffle-test pipeline already produces (per-user shuffle delta + sequential-rule coverage + order-aware/agnostic gap), with NO new per-user labels, passed through a semi-synthetic-probe calibration c(.); C3 -- TWO orthogonal knobs act on the SAME calibrated field: a stratification/routing gate (gamma) and a reweighting (phi); C4 -- an EXACT-COLLAPSE strict generalization (E_collapse <= 1e-6) back to the dataset-level shuffle verdict S(D).

BOUNDARY RECOVERIES (named baseline => exact special point of the field) [provable-by-construction]:

  R1 Global shuffle test (the source paper [1]): s_cal(u) == S(D) constant => single stratum, uniform weights, constant router => PRISM reports exactly S(D) and pooled M. This is the deployed baseline and the COLLAPSE TARGET.
  R2 Sub-population selection (evaluate only on "sequential users"): hard threshold gamma with KNOB2 off (phi == 1) => stratified/selected metric on { u : s_cal(u) >= gamma }.
  R3 Reweighted-only global metric: KNOB1 off (single stratum, no routing) => sum_u w(u) M_u / sum_u w(u), a sequentiality-controlled pooled number.
  R4 Popularity-for-everyone (the s -> 0 limit): gamma above max_u s_cal(u) => rho(u) = pop for all u (order-agnostic recommender).
  R5 Oracle field: s_hat replaced by the planted semi-synthetic per-user labels => upper-bound ceiling of the field.

Load-bearing symbols: D dataset; U test users; u user; t position in a history; i item; H_u = (i_1,...,i_{n_u}) user u's chronological history; n_u its length; pi a sequential model (SASRec/GRU4Rec/BERT4Rec); pi_bag its order-agnostic twin (sequence block -> mean-pool/feedforward); pop a popularity/order-agnostic recommender; M_u(pi, H) a per-user ranking metric (NDCG@k/HitRate@k); M(pi, D) its pooled mean; shuffle(.) a random permutation of a history; S(D) the global relative drop (source-paper verdict); s(u) in [0,1] the per-user order-relevance field; s(u,t) its per-interaction refinement; s_hat(u) the free estimate (fusing e1,e2,e3); c(.) the probe calibration map; s_cal(u) = c(s_hat(u)); gamma stratify/route threshold; rho(u) the per-user router; phi reweight map; w(u) = phi(s_cal(u)) per-user weight; E_collapse exact-collapse budget; epsilon do-no-harm margin; keep_rate / stratum sizes the selection-control quantities; homogeneous default = disable estimation (s_cal(u) == S(D)).

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Does promoting a dataset's sequential structure from a single global shuffle-based scalar S(D) to a calibrated per-user (and per-interaction) order-relevance field s(u) -- estimated for free from per-user shuffle deltas, sequential-rule coverage, and an order-aware-versus-order-agnostic predictability gap, calibrated on a semi-synthetic probe, and used to stratify evaluation, route users between sequential and order-agnostic models, and reweight training -- yield a more faithful assessment of dataset suitability and higher recommendation accuracy than the dataset-level verdict, while collapsing exactly to that verdict at the homogeneous default?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

P1 (the status quo it disrupts: a global, dataset-level constant). The field has just acquired its first instrument for the unexamined assumption that benchmarks are sequential -- the shuffle test of "Does It Look Sequential?" [1], building on order-ablation [7], retrain-on-shuffled [8], and rank-sensitivity [9]. That instrument reports ONE number per dataset (e.g. SASRec NDCG@10 drops -10% on Steam, -59% on ML-20m [1]) and thereby asserts that sequentiality is a property of the dataset as a whole. PRISM disrupts exactly this constant: POOL shows S(D) is merely the population MEAN of a per-user field s(u), so the community has been characterizing benchmarks -- and deciding which to keep -- with a statistic that is blind to its own variance.

P2 (the silent harm: a mean hides the mixture, and the mixture is the point). A pooled drop cannot distinguish a uniformly mildly-sequential dataset from a strongly-sequential minority drowned in a non-sequential majority, yet these call for opposite actions. The first-order consequence is a DATASET-SELECTION error in both directions: a benchmark discarded as "weakly sequential" (Steam, RetailRocket, Yelp, Gowalla, Foursquare [1]) discards, with it, a possibly large sub-population of genuinely sequential users for whom that data is perfectly valid; a benchmark endorsed as "strongly sequential" (ML-20m [1]) invites sequential models to be tuned and credited on a non-sequential remainder where order is irrelevant. Because every downstream paper inherits the global verdict, the error is a multiplier on the literature, and it is INVISIBLE under the pooled metrics those papers report (metric blindness). The detection signals already exist in the source pipeline -- per-user shuffle deltas, per-user rule coverage, per-user order-aware/agnostic gaps -- but are averaged away before anyone looks.

P3 (why it advances knowledge: an unmeasured population becomes a calibrated, inspectable field). The contribution is epistemic before it is empirical. PRISM turns the unmeasured assumption (one sequentiality per dataset) into a calibrated, inspectable per-user quantity s_cal(u) = c(s_hat(u)), and reframes the community's question from "IS this dataset sequential?" to "WHICH users (and which interactions) in it are sequential, and does my model exploit exactly those?" -- a per-sample, stratifiable question. It yields a reusable SEQUENTIALITY-PROFILE protocol: instead of a scalar verdict, each dataset gets a distribution (its mean recovers S(D), but its skew, bimodality, and tail mass are new, decision-relevant descriptors), and any future benchmark or augmentation can be audited with it. Naming s and measuring its SHAPE is the durable knowledge; the two knobs are the first thing one does once the field exists.

P4 (practical payoff: free, drop-in, risk-free, and immediately actionable). The estimator signals are cheap: e1 reuses the source paper's no-retrain shuffle of test sequences [1], read per user; e3 is a byproduct of a single forward pass of pi and its bag twin [7]; e2 is the model-agnostic rule mining [1] tallied per user. PRISM is therefore model-agnostic (SASRec [2], GRU4Rec [3], BERT4Rec [4]) and drops into an existing evaluation harness as a per-user annotation plus a stratified/reweighted/routed readout. Adoption is risk-free by construction: disabling estimation (s_cal(u) == S(D)) reproduces the dataset-level shuffle verdict readout-for-readout with E_collapse <= 1e-6, removing the usual barrier to changing a protocol, while a do-no-harm margin epsilon underwrites the near-homogeneous regime. And the payoff is concrete: per-user ROUTING (sequential model for sequential users, popularity/EASE for the rest) is a deployable accuracy and efficiency win that a single global model leaves on the table.

P5 (the stakes: the dominant axis of the line is "which datasets, which models," and PRISM sharpens both). "Does It Look Sequential?" matters because dataset choice propagates into every SRS comparison; PRISM matters more because it protects that choice at the granularity where the decision actually lives. A community that can say "Steam is 12% strongly-sequential users and 88% noise" can keep Steam FOR the right sub-population instead of discarding it wholesale, can report model gains stratified by where order matters (ending the inflation of pooled NDCG by a sequential tail), and can route deployments to the cheapest sufficient model per user. Closing POOL targets the exact quantity the source paper introduced -- sequential structure -- at the resolution that makes it decision-grade, for free, with a provable safety net.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 CIRCULARITY [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Two estimator channels (e1 per-user shuffle delta, e3 order-aware/agnostic gap) use a MODEL to judge a user. NAIVE FAILS: a model that has not learned to use order will report a small shuffle drop -- and thus low s_hat(u) -- even for a genuinely sequential user, so the field measures "does THIS model use order for u," not "does order matter for u," and the router would then deny sequential treatment to exactly the users who need it. REPRESENTATIONAL because the fix is a structural choice about WHERE the scoring signal comes from. MITIGATION: anchor on the model-agnostic channel e2; compute e1/e3 with a CROSS-FIT or FROZEN teacher trained on a disjoint user/interaction fold, never the live evaluated model; calibrate against the semi-synthetic oracle. Falsified by the PLACEBO rung; bounded by the ORACLE rung.

O2 NO PER-USER GROUND-TRUTH LABEL [REPRESENTATIONAL]. KNOB1's threshold and KNOB2's weight need s_cal(u) to behave like Pr[order is informative for u], but no dataset ships a per-user "is sequential" label. NAIVE FAILS: raw per-user shuffle deltas are uncalibrated and not comparable across users or datasets, so thresholding them conflates magnitude artifacts with order-relevance. REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces a calibration map c(.) into the architecture -- s_hat is never used directly; only s_cal = c(s_hat) drives the knobs. MITIGATION: fit c(.) on a SEMI-SYNTHETIC PROBE with planted per-user labels (Markov-chain sequential users mixed with i.i.d.-popularity non-sequential users) via isotonic/Platt; anchor so the population mean of s_cal equals S(D) (exact collapse); report ECE(s_cal) far below ECE(raw s_hat).

O3 SHORT-SEQUENCE / SMALL-SAMPLE VARIANCE. This is the central statistical obstacle. A per-user metric is computed on very few held-out targets -- often one -- so a single hit/miss makes the per-user shuffle delta almost a Bernoulli coin flip. NAIVE FAILS: thresholding a one-target per-user drop yields a near-random field, and any "stratified" result is then noise sorted into bins. MITIGATION: average over MANY shuffle permutations per user; leave-one-out over multiple targets where n_u permits; treat s(u) as a POSTERIOR via empirical-Bayes / hierarchical shrinkage toward the dataset mean S(D) (so low-evidence users are pulled to the global prior rather than trusted); and report per-stratum RELIABILITY. The hierarchical field is what makes per-user claims defensible.

O4 SHUFFLE CONFOUND -- ORDER vs. RECENCY/POSITION [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Shuffling destroys not only long-range sequential dependency but also RECENCY (which item is last) and absolute position. NAIVE FAILS: a user whose drop is driven purely by "the next item repeats the most-recent one" (a repeat-consumption dynamic [22]) is scored as "sequential" though only recency, not order, matters -- and a popularity-plus-recency model would serve them as well as a sequential one. REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces the null INTO the design: s(u) must be defined against the appropriate perturbation. MITIGATION: a battery of position-controlled perturbations (full shuffle, shuffle-all-but-last, block shuffle, adjacent-swap) decomposes order-relevance from recency; the per-interaction field s(u,t) localizes WHERE in the history order matters; report the decomposition rather than a single drop.

O5 SELECTION / KEEP-RATE CONFOUND [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Routing and stratifying change WHICH users a model is scored on, and "we evaluated on the sequential stratum and the sequential model looked better" can be a pure selection effect. NAIVE FAILS: a raw "filter-and-improve" result cannot separate the gain from MEASURING order-relevance (the claim) from the gain of scoring on an easier/sparser sub-population (a confound). REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces a control into the design: stratum sizes and keep_rate become first-class reported quantities. MITIGATION: a rate-matched PLACEBO (a random field with the SAME stratum sizes) must be beaten beyond seed noise; routing is judged by REGRET against an oracle router; every success number is reported with its stratum size.

O6 METRIC BLINDNESS / AGGREGATION [REPRESENTATIONAL]. Pooled NDCG averages over the field, so both the phenomenon (POOL) and the fix (PRISM) are invisible in the very metric the field reports by default. NAIVE FAILS: reporting only the pooled mean hides the mixture and makes the contribution unobservable. REPRESENTATIONAL because it forces the EVALUATION's structure: the primary readouts must be STRATIFIED, and the field's SHAPE (skew, bimodality coefficient, tail mass above a threshold) must be reported, since the method's value is the gradient of model behavior across strata, not a shift in the mean.

O7 CONFOUND WITH SEQUENCE LENGTH / ACTIVITY [auxiliary]. Longer, more active users both have more order to exploit and yield lower-variance estimates, so s(u) risks being a proxy for n_u. MITIGATION: length-matched strata and partial correlation controlling for n_u; PASS only if residual order-relevance survives length control (a sequential SHORT user and a non-sequential LONG user must be separable).

WHY IT IS NONETHELESS TRACTABLE. Every obstacle is matched by a structural feature already in the frame: circularity (O1) is fenced by the model-agnostic channel e2 plus a cross-fit/frozen teacher; the missing-label problem (O2) is solved by a semi-synthetic probe rather than per-user annotation; small-sample variance (O3) is absorbed by empirical-Bayes shrinkage to S(D); the shuffle confound (O4) is separated by position-controlled nulls and the per-interaction field; the selection (O5) and aggregation (O6) confounds are neutralized by the rate-matched placebo and the mandated stratified readout; and the length confound (O7) by length control. Above all, the EXACT-COLLAPSE identity (s_cal(u) == S(D) => PRISM == shuffle test, E_collapse <= 1e-6) pins the downside to zero, so the entire research risk is on the UPSIDE.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

Prior art falls into five families; for each we give representative work, what it does, the precise delta PRISM adds, and the boundary recovery it maps to.

(1) Shuffle / order-perturbation dataset tests -- "Does It Look Sequential?" [1], retrain-on-shuffled [8], rank-list sensitivity [9], and perturbation-robustness analyses of SRSs [12]. WHAT: shuffle or perturb user histories and measure the DATASET-LEVEL drop in a metric or the global top-K Jaccard. DELTA: all report a global scalar S(D); none promotes it to a per-user field, estimates that field for free, calibrates it, or acts on it. PRISM adds the full wedge C1-C4 and recovers these exactly as R1 (s_cal == S(D) => PRISM reports S(D)).

(2) Order-ablation / architecture-replacement tests -- the sequential-vs-feedforward comparison of Hidasi and Czapp [7]. WHAT: compare a sequential model to its bag-of-items twin at the dataset level to see whether the sequence block earns its keep. DELTA: this is a single global contrast; PRISM folds it in as ONE per-user estimator channel (e3) and unifies it with shuffle (e1) and rules (e2) on a calibrated field. Used globally it maps to R3 (reweight-only with a constant field).

(3) Dataset characterization / selection -- dataset-descriptor clustering [16], data-splitting and protocol studies [15, 13], rigorous-benchmarking critiques [14, 17]. WHAT: describe or select whole datasets by global statistics, or fix splits/leakage. DELTA: these operate at dataset granularity and on the PROTOCOL, not on per-user order-relevance of the SIGNAL; none produces a within-dataset field. PRISM is orthogonal and complementary: it characterizes the INTERIOR of a dataset.

(4) Sequential models and their revisions -- SASRec [2], GRU4Rec [3], BERT4Rec [4], Caser [5], and replicability/loss/calibration revisions [10, 11, 19]. WHAT: build or re-evaluate sequential architectures, reporting pooled metrics. DELTA: they consume datasets as given and report means; PRISM tells them WHERE their sequential capacity is actually used, enabling per-user routing to an order-agnostic baseline (R4, the s->0 limit) where it is not.

(5) Calibration and uncertainty -- isotonic/Platt scaling and modern-network calibration [18]. WHAT: map raw scores to calibrated probabilities. DELTA: calibration has never been applied to a per-user SEQUENTIALITY estimate against a known-sequentiality probe; PRISM uses it as the component c(.) that makes s_hat comparable and thresholdable, with the anchor that yields exact collapse.

THE TWO BARRIERS THAT KEPT IT UNSOLVED. (a) It LOOKED like it needed per-user labels: judging whether order matters for a single user seems to require a per-user ground truth that no benchmark provides, and per-user shuffle deltas are far too noisy to threshold raw (O3). The UNLOCK is twofold -- estimate the field for FREE by reading the source pipeline's own signals per user (e1, e2, e3), and CALIBRATE/SHRINK them with a semi-synthetic probe and empirical-Bayes, so no per-user annotation is ever required. (b) Sequentiality was BORN as a dataset-level question: the shuffle test arrived in 2024 [1] as a per-dataset verdict, and the entire surrounding literature reports pooled metrics, so "which users" was never posed. PRISM poses it, and supplies the exact-collapse recovery of the dataset-level verdict (R1) that lets the field be adopted without abandoning the scalar it generalizes.

ONE CLEAN NOVELTY SENTENCE. PRISM is the first method to model a dataset's sequential structure as a calibrated, free-to-estimate per-user (and per-interaction) order-relevance field, and to act on it with BOTH a stratification/routing gate and a reweighting, while provably collapsing to the dataset-level shuffle verdict at the homogeneous default (E_collapse <= 1e-6).

STRONGEST PRIOR-ART RISK (flagged honestly). The most dangerous neighbor is the obvious "just look at the per-user shuffle drop" reaction -- a reviewer could argue that computing a per-user version of the source paper's metric is trivial. PRISM's defensible deltas are three and concrete: (i) raw per-user drops are NOT usable -- they are uncalibrated and dominated by short-sequence Bernoulli noise (O3), and PRISM's load-bearing content is the CALIBRATION (semi-synthetic probe c(.)) and SHRINKAGE (empirical-Bayes to S(D)) that turn noise into a thresholdable field; (ii) UNIFICATION -- the same calibrated field fuses three heterogeneous channels (shuffle, rules, order-aware/agnostic gap) and drives BOTH knobs, where a naive per-user drop is a single noisy channel driving nothing; (iii) the CONFOUND CONTROLS -- the rate-matched placebo (O5), position-controlled nulls separating order from recency (O4), and length control (O7) are exactly what a naive per-user drop lacks, and the EXACT-COLLAPSE recovery (R1) is what makes it a strict generalization rather than a different metric. The novelty is not "shuffle per user" -- it is MEASURING per-user order-relevance as a calibrated, confound-controlled field and ACTING on it under a provable safety net.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD PIPELINE (end-to-end). Step 0 Inputs: any benchmark D and any sequential model pi (SASRec/GRU4Rec/BERT4Rec) plus its order-agnostic twin pi_bag and a popularity/EASE baseline. Step 1 Estimate s_hat(u) FOR FREE from e1 per-user shuffle delta (relative per-user metric drop under shuffling only u's test history, using a CROSS-FIT/FROZEN teacher, averaged over multiple permutations), e2 per-user sequential-rule coverage (fraction of u's adjacent transitions covered by mined rules with lift over popularity/Markov-0), and e3 per-user order-aware/agnostic predictability gap (per-user log-prob or reciprocal-rank difference between pi and pi_bag [7]); fuse by a simple monotone combiner. Step 2 Calibrate: s_cal(u) = c(s_hat(u)), c(.) fit by isotonic/Platt on a SEMI-SYNTHETIC PROBE with planted per-user sequential/non-sequential labels, anchored so the metric-weighted mean of s_cal equals S(D). Step 3 Shrink: replace s_cal(u) by its empirical-Bayes posterior toward S(D) for low-evidence (short) users (O3). Step 4 KNOB1: stratify the test users by s_cal(u) and report metrics per stratum (PRIMARY readout); optionally route rho(u) = pi if s_cal(u) >= gamma else pi_bag/pop. Step 5 KNOB2: report the reweighted sequentiality-controlled metric sum_u w(u) M_u / sum_u w(u) with w(u) = phi(s_cal(u)), and optionally reweight training. Safety rails fold in: cross-fit teacher (O1), semi-synthetic calibration (O2), shrinkage (O3), position-controlled nulls + per-interaction s(u,t) (O4), rate-matched placebo + stratum-size reporting (O5), stratified primary readout (O6), length control (O7), and the EXACT-COLLAPSE check (s_cal(u) == S(D) => PRISM == shuffle test, E_collapse <= 1e-6).

DATASETS / BENCHMARKS (all from the source study, plus a controlled probe). The 15 datasets of "Does It Look Sequential?" [1] spanning weak-to-strong global sequentiality: Amazon-Beauty/Sports/Games [via 17-style Amazon data], Diginetica, Yoochoose, RetailRocket, OTTO, MegaMarket, Steam, ML-20m, 30Music, Zvuk, Foursquare, Gowalla, Yelp; PLUS a family of SEMI-SYNTHETIC MIXTURES with a known per-user sequential fraction (Markov-chain sequential users blended in controlled proportions with i.i.d.-popularity users) that serve as the ORACLE and let us plant a field of known shape. Preprocessing follows the source paper (5-core, single event type, repeated-item removal) [1] for comparability.

MODELS / BACKBONES. Sequential: SASRec [2], GRU4Rec [3], BERT4Rec [4]. Order-agnostic twins/baselines: a bag SASRec (mean-pooled, sequence block -> feedforward [7]), item-KNN, EASE [20]/SLIM [21], and most-popular. The field is backbone-agnostic; routing chooses between pi and an order-agnostic recommender per user.

BASELINES = boundary recoveries R1..R5 + a strong competitor. R1 global shuffle test [1] -- the named status quo and COLLAPSE TARGET; R2 hard sub-population selection (evaluate on s_cal >= gamma); R3 reweight-only global metric (KNOB1 off); R4 popularity-for-everyone (s->0 limit); R5 oracle field on semi-synthetic data (ceiling); PLUS a strong global model-selection baseline ("use SASRec iff S(D) is large") to show that PER-USER routing beats the PER-DATASET decision the source verdict implies.

METRICS. (a) Recommendation accuracy: NDCG@k, HitRate@k, MRR, pooled AND stratified by s_cal(u) (stratified is PRIMARY); per-user top-K Jaccard [9] under shuffling. (b) Field quality: AUROC and ECE of s_cal vs. oracle on semi-synthetic data, and of calibrated vs. raw s_hat (to show calibration is load-bearing); the FIELD SHAPE per real dataset (mean -- which must recover S(D) -- skew, bimodality coefficient, fraction of users with drop >= tau). (c) Routing: accuracy lift over the best single global model, and REGRET vs. the oracle router. (d) Controls: stratum sizes / keep_rate with every number; partial correlation of s(u) with n_u (O7); E_collapse (CI-gateable, <= 1e-6).

ABLATION GRID. Orthogonal {KNOB1 on/off} x {KNOB2 on/off} (recovers R1, R2, R3, full PRISM at the corners); estimator channels {e1, e2, e3 and all combos} x {raw s_hat vs. calibrated c(.)}; teacher source {in-loop, frozen, cross-fit} to expose circularity (O1); permutation count and shrinkage strength to expose short-sequence variance (O3); perturbation type {full / all-but-last / block / adjacent-swap} to expose the recency confound (O4); gamma sweep (endpoints recover R1 single-stratum and R4 popularity-for-all).

FALSIFIER-LADDER RUNS (explicit PASS conditions). ORACLE (ceiling, R5): on semi-synthetic mixtures with planted per-user labels, PASS if s_cal recovers the field (high AUROC) AND PRISM routing approaches the oracle router; FALSIFIES (estimator) if free s_hat is no better than PLACEBO. PLACEBO (rate-matched random field): PASS if PRISM's stratified/routing lift exceeds the placebo's at matched stratum sizes by more than seed noise; FALSIFIES if the gain was merely splitting/reweighting data. RECOVERY (reproduces baseline): s_cal(u) == S(D) => PRISM readouts equal the shuffle-test readouts, E_collapse <= 1e-6; FALSIFIES (construction) on any deviation > 1e-6. LEAKAGE: the semi-synthetic probe and the cross-fit fold are disjoint from the evaluation users; s_hat never reads eval targets; PASS if gains survive the strict split. CONFOUND: length-matched strata and recency-controlled nulls; PASS if residual order-relevance survives length control (O7) and if order is separable from recency (O4); FALSIFIES if s(u) collapses to a length or recency proxy.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES (as HYPOTHESES; provable items flagged). H1 [conjectured->measured]: the per-user field is heavily right-skewed / bimodal, NOT a point mass -- "weakly sequential" datasets (Steam, RetailRocket, Yelp, Gowalla, Foursquare [1]) contain a non-trivial strongly-sequential tail, and "strongly sequential" datasets (ML-20m [1]) contain a substantial non-sequential mass; the field mean recovers each dataset's published S(D). H2 [conjectured->measured]: sequential-model gains over order-agnostic baselines CONCENTRATE in high-s_cal users, so the gradient of lift across strata is itself the evidence that the field measures real heterogeneity (POOL is real). H3 [conjectured->measured]: per-user ROUTING by s_cal beats both the best single global model and the per-dataset "SASRec iff S(D) large" decision on pooled accuracy, and approaches the oracle router. H4 [conjectured->measured]: s(u) correlates with but is not reducible to sequence length -- residual order-relevance survives length control (O7). H5 [conjectured->measured]: calibrated s_cal far exceeds AUROC 0.5 against oracle labels and has ECE well below raw s_hat; a cross-fit teacher beats an in-loop teacher (confirming circularity O1 is real). P1 [provable-by-construction]: EXACT-COLLAPSE -- s_cal(u) == S(D) => PRISM == shuffle test, E_collapse <= 1e-6. P2 [provable-by-construction]: boundary recoveries R1..R5 are exact special points of the field.

RESPONSIBLE EVALUATION AND DEPLOYMENT CONSIDERATIONS. (1) Decision-support framing: PRISM annotates datasets and routes models; it is a measurement and decision-support tool, not an autonomous judge -- a dataset should not be discarded OR endorsed on s alone, and the field's SHAPE (not a new scalar) is the reported object. (2) Confidently-wrong s_cal: a c(.) fit on easy semi-synthetic mixtures can mis-rank real users at distribution shift, so we report ECE separately on the synthetic probe and on any human/-sim-validated real subset, and route conservatively (high gamma) until calibration transfer is confirmed. (3) Short-sequence honesty: per-user claims on very short histories are low-evidence by construction (O3); we shrink them to the dataset prior and report per-stratum reliability rather than over-trusting individual users. (4) Fairness of routing: routing must not systematically deny quality to a user group; we audit routed accuracy across activity and length bands and require non-inferiority within epsilon for low-s_cal users (who are sent to the order-agnostic model). (5) Confound discipline: stratum sizes / keep_rate are REQUIRED alongside every success number, order is reported separately from recency (O4), and all H1-H5 are explicitly HYPOTHESES -- only the EXACT-COLLAPSE identity and boundary recoveries are provable, and they do not imply empirical gains. (6) Scope: claims are scoped to next-item top-K evaluation on the tested datasets; session-based, repeat-consumption-dominated, and strongly time-interval-dependent regimes are flagged as separate validation targets.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] A. Klenitskiy, A. Volodkevich, A. Pembek, A. Vasilev. "Does It Look Sequential? An Analysis of Datasets for Evaluation of Sequential Recommendations." RecSys '24 (arXiv:2408.12008). DOI: 10.1145/3640457.3688195. Code/appendix: github.com/Antondfger/Does-It-Look-Sequential. -- SOURCE PAPER; 15 datasets; three shuffle-based measures (sequential rules, SASRec/GRU4Rec NDCG@k & HitRate@k drop on shuffled test sequences, top-K Jaccard); a bootstrap significance test flags six datasets (Diginetica, Foursquare, Gowalla, RetailRocket, Steam, Yelp) as weakly sequential (e.g. SASRec NDCG@10 drop -4% RetailRocket, -10% Steam, ~+5% Yelp, vs -59% ML-20m).
[2] W.-C. Kang, J. McAuley. "Self-Attentive Sequential Recommendation" (SASRec). ICDM 2018 (arXiv:1808.09781).
[3] B. Hidasi, A. Karatzoglou, L. Baltrunas, D. Tikk. "Session-based Recommendations with Recurrent Neural Networks" (GRU4Rec). ICLR 2016 (arXiv:1511.06939).
[4] F. Sun, J. Liu, J. Wu, C. Pei, X. Lin, W. Ou, P. Jiang. "BERT4Rec: Sequential Recommendation with Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformer." CIKM 2019 (arXiv:1904.06690).
[5] J. Tang, K. Wang. "Personalized Top-N Sequential Recommendation via Convolutional Sequence Embedding" (Caser). WSDM 2018 (arXiv:1809.07426). -- analyzes union-level sequential patterns and skip behavior via sequential association rules.
[6] M. Quadrana, P. Cremonesi, D. Jannach. "Sequence-Aware Recommender Systems." ACM Computing Surveys 51(4), 2018 (arXiv:1802.08452).
[7] B. Hidasi, A. T. Czapp. "Widespread Flaws in Offline Evaluation of Recommender Systems." RecSys 2023 (arXiv:2307.14951; DOI: 10.1145/3604915.3608839). -- names dataset-task mismatch; compares a sequential model to a feedforward (order-agnostic) variant; basis of estimator channel e3.
[8] D. Woolridge, S. Wilner, M. Glick. "Sequence or Pseudo-Sequence? An Analysis of Sequential Recommendation Datasets." Perspectives on the Evaluation of Recommender Systems Workshop (PERSPECTIVES @ RecSys), 2021. -- retrain-on-shuffled shuffle test with SASRec.
[9] S. Oh, B. Ustun, J. McAuley, S. Kumar. "Rank List Sensitivity of Recommender Systems to Interaction Perturbations." CIKM 2022 (arXiv:2201.12686). -- top-K Jaccard Rank List Sensitivity, adapted by [1] to shuffling.
[10] A. Petrov, C. Macdonald. "A Systematic Review and Replicability Study of BERT4Rec for Sequential Recommendation." RecSys 2022 (arXiv:2207.07483).
[11] A. Klenitskiy, A. Vasilev. "Turning Dross Into Gold Loss: Is BERT4Rec Really Better Than SASRec?" RecSys 2023 (arXiv:2309.07602).
[12] F. Betello, F. Siciliano, P. Mishra, F. Silvestri. "Investigating the Robustness of Sequential Recommender Systems Against Training Data Perturbations." ECIR 2024 (arXiv:2307.13165). -- perturbation-based robustness analysis of SRSs; the Betello et al. work cited by [1] in the rank-sensitivity context.
[13] Y. Ji, A. Sun, J. Zhang, C. Li. "A Critical Study on Data Leakage in Recommender System Offline Evaluation." ACM Trans. Inf. Syst. 41(3), Article 75, 2023 (DOI: 10.1145/3569930).
[14] Z. Sun, et al. "Are We Evaluating Rigorously? Benchmarking Recommendation for Reproducible Evaluation and Fair Comparison." RecSys 2020.
[15] Z. Meng, R. McCreadie, C. Macdonald, I. Ounis. "Exploring Data Splitting Strategies for the Evaluation of Recommendation Models." RecSys 2020 (arXiv:2007.13237).
[16] J. Y. Chin, Y. Chen, G. Cong. "The Datasets Dilemma: How Much Do We Really Know About Recommendation Datasets?" WSDM 2022. -- dataset-descriptor characterization and selection of diverse datasets.
[17] M. Ferrari Dacrema, P. Cremonesi, D. Jannach. "Are We Really Making Much Progress? A Worrying Analysis of Recent Neural Recommendation Approaches." RecSys 2019 (arXiv:1907.06902; DOI: 10.1145/3298689.3347058).
[18] C. Guo, G. Pleiss, Y. Sun, K. Q. Weinberger. "On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks." ICML 2017 (arXiv:1706.04599). -- Platt/temperature/isotonic calibration; basis of c(.).
[19] A. V. Petrov, C. Macdonald. "gSASRec: Reducing Overconfidence in Sequential Recommendation Trained with Negative Sampling." RecSys 2023 (arXiv:2308.07192; DOI: 10.1145/3604915.3608783). -- overconfidence/calibration in sequential models.
[20] H. Steck. "Embarrassingly Shallow Autoencoders for Sparse Data" (EASE). WWW 2019 (arXiv:1905.03375). -- strong order-agnostic baseline for routing.
[21] X. Ning, G. Karypis. "SLIM: Sparse Linear Methods for Top-N Recommender Systems." ICDM 2011. -- order-agnostic linear baseline.
[22] A. Anderson, R. Kumar, A. Tomkins, S. Vassilvitskii. "The Dynamics of Repeat Consumption." WWW 2014. -- evidence that order/recency relevance varies sharply across users and items.
[23] W. X. Zhao, et al. "RecBole: Towards a Unified, Comprehensive and Efficient Framework for Recommendation Algorithms." CIKM 2021 (arXiv:2011.01731). -- reference implementations for reproducible SRS evaluation.
[24] R. Canamares, P. Castells. "Should I Follow the Crowd? A Probabilistic Analysis of the Effectiveness of Popularity in Recommender Systems." SIGIR 2018. -- popularity strength, motivating the order-agnostic routing target.

EVIDENCE BASE. The diagnosis rests on the source paper's own published results: the three shuffle-based measures are all dataset-level scalars [1], and the cross-dataset spread of the SASRec NDCG@10 drop (-4% to -59% [1, Table 2]) plus the inverted top-K Jaccard spread (0.12 to 0.59 [1]) are direct evidence that order-relevance is highly variable -- across datasets demonstrably, and (the hypothesis this proposal makes testable) within them. The estimator channels are not new instruments but the source pipeline's own signals read per user (e1 = its no-retrain test-shuffle [1], e2 = its rule mining [1], e3 = the feedforward-replacement ablation [7]); the calibration component is standard [18]; and the order-agnostic routing targets [20, 21, 24] and reproducible harnesses [23] already exist. PRISM's contribution is to fuse, calibrate, shrink, and act on these per-user signals under an exact-collapse guarantee back to "Does It Look Sequential?" [1].
